Software quality does not occur accidentally; it requires cultivating a strong quality culture within the project team. This culture is shaped by continuous planning, monitoring, and asking the right questions throughout the project life cycle. Project managers must repeatedly evaluate whether schedules are being met, whether too many defects are being discovered, and whether testing is progressing at the desired pace. Agile teams often become adept at answering such questions due to close collaboration with customers and frequent iteration. Metrics—such as defects per KLOC, lines of code per person-month, and test case execution rates—offer objective indicators of project performance. They help managers assess quality, identify problem areas, anticipate risks, and make informed decisions. Targets must be set early to ensure metrics are useful; for example, if 50 test cases are expected in week one, the number executed provides immediate insight into progress. Without metrics, it becomes extremely difficult to understand how a project is performing or to improve future development efforts.

Internal quality refers to the quality-focused activities completed by the development team itself. Cultivating an internal quality mindset requires establishing shared responsibility, team pride, and disciplined engineering habits. Quality emerges when leadership encourages the team to ensure their work is orderly, correct, and aligned with project priorities. Internal quality includes activities such as following documented procedures, complying with standards, and maintaining consistent artifacts throughout the development life cycle. These habits are reinforced through the project management plan, the requirements document, the design, and coding conventions. External quality, in contrast, is handled through verification and validation plans that are documented separately. The coordination between internal and external quality activities is illustrated through planned inspections, unit testing, and system testing. In many teams, each member has primary and backup responsibilities to ensure continuity. Leaders promote quality by setting expectations and fostering collaboration, and backups can act as pair inspectors to maintain thoroughness. Altogether, internal quality management is as much a cultural effort as it is a technical process.

During project planning, teams determine which metrics will be collected and how they will be used. Some metrics apply across all phases of the project—such as total defects—while others apply only during specific activities, such as test case execution. Useful metrics include project milestones, testing progress, defect detection rate, and defect resolution rate. Milestones provide concrete objectives that define when specific deliverables must be completed, and managers monitor progress against these dates to assess schedule accuracy. Testing progress focuses on the number of executed versus planned test cases. Defect injection and detection metrics help determine when defects were introduced and when they were found, which offers insight into the maturity and stability of the development process. Metrics like these help project managers evaluate whether testing is on schedule, whether defect trends indicate deeper structural issues, and whether additional investigation is needed. Understanding where defects originate provides critical insight into design quality, requirements clarity, and coding practices.

Defect metrics are one of the most widely used indicators of product quality. Defects often occur in one phase of development but may not be discovered until much later, making it important to record both injection and detection phases. A defect detection rate of 0.2 per 100 requirements defects in the implementation phase would indicate that one defect out of every five hundred requirements was found during coding. By comparing the number of defects detected within each phase to organizational norms, project teams can assess whether their development process is effective or unusually error-prone. For example, if only two defects per 100 requirements were detected during the requirements phase when the organization’s average is five, this suggests that inspection quality may be higher than usual. Conversely, unusual patterns—such as fewer design defects being detected during inspections but higher defect counts later—signal the need for additional investigation. Tracking detection versus injection phases helps identify weaknesses in earlier stages of the project and provides a basis for process improvement activities.

Defect resolution metrics measure how quickly issues are resolved once they are identified, providing insight into the overall maturity and responsiveness of the development team. Even if detection metrics meet expectations, a low defect resolution rate can indicate a growing backlog that threatens schedules and quality. Managers track weekly counts of submitted, resolved, and remaining defects to identify trends. For example, if the number of open defects is substantially higher than planned by the end of a particular week, corrective actions are needed. Project dashboards can summarize these trends using graphical indicators similar to automobile dashboards, allowing stakeholders to quickly understand project health. Dashboards commonly include metrics such as earned value, cost variance, schedule variance, quality indicators, and productivity levels. By monitoring these metrics, teams can take proactive action, such as reallocating resources, improving inspections, or modifying schedules. Effective monitoring ensures that the team adapts to problems early, rather than discovering issues too late to correct them efficiently.

Once a project has identified the set of metrics to collect, the next step is to establish realistic targets for each metric. Planning begins by analyzing historical data from previous projects and using these results as a baseline. Without such baselines, it becomes nearly impossible for project managers to determine whether a project is meeting expectations. For example, if the organization’s normal defect rate for the requirements phase is 5 per 100 requirements, and the current project reveals a rate of only 2, this may signal increased process maturity or unusually effective inspections. The project manager can then adjust schedules, expectations, or workload based on these findings. Weekly reviews of defect submission and resolution rates help track trends and highlight areas that need attention. When open defects remain significantly higher than anticipated, the project manager must decide whether to redirect resources, adjust deadlines, or refine the testing process. Proper planning ensures that metrics serve as decision-making tools rather than merely reports. Ultimately, metric-based planning strengthens quality management by enabling proactive, data-driven responses throughout the project lifecycle.

As the project progresses, it is essential for key team members to regularly review the collected metrics. These reviews typically occur weekly and provide an opportunity to evaluate project health. Charts, tables, and dashboards are often used to summarize large amounts of information concisely. A dashboard presents a graphical overview of metrics such as earned value, cost variance, task completion rates, defect trends, and quality indicators. Much like a car dashboard displays speed and fuel levels at a glance, a project dashboard gives stakeholders a quick understanding of overall performance. For example, if defect resolution falls behind schedule, the dashboard might highlight an increasing backlog or flag areas where defect rates exceed planned thresholds. Additional charts may track open issues by activity, code quality by module, or risk exposure levels. While dashboards provide valuable summaries, they are not a replacement for detailed analysis. Project managers must still drill down into individual defects, requirement changes, or configuration updates when unusual patterns appear. Regular metric reviews ensure the team identifies problems early, adjusts processes promptly, and maintains the quality and predictability required for successful project delivery.

Metrics serve not only to measure performance but also to guide improvements within a project. Teams compare current data against organizational norms and previous phases to determine whether their process is trending upward or downward. For example, Table 9.2 provides a summary of typical data collected across research, drafting, reviewing, and post-mortem activities. The team may observe that drafting required more hours than usual, or that review productivity dropped compared to past projects. These variations prompt discussions about root causes. Perhaps a new tool slowed the drafting process, or the team underestimated document complexity. If the review process consumed fewer resources than expected, the team might conclude that additional inspection time would have been beneficial. Improvement actions are documented at the end of each phase so they can be applied during the next iteration. This structured reflection enables continuous improvement. Over time, teams learn which practices consistently yield better quality—such as spending more effort on requirements analysis or adjusting inspection techniques. Metrics thus become a feedback loop, allowing teams to refine their workflow and make evidence-based adjustments throughout the project.

Organizations committed to long-term success analyze metrics not only within individual projects but also across multiple projects. By comparing results, trends emerge that reveal strengths and weaknesses at the organizational level. For example, one project may show high schedule predictability but poor design stability, while another may exhibit excellent testing efficiency yet struggle with requirements volatility. By identifying common patterns across projects, companies can develop targeted improvement strategies. Steps typically include identifying important improvement areas such as quality, schedule adherence, or efficiency; collecting baseline metrics; and defining processes to achieve measurable progress. Improvement goals may focus on reducing defects per KLOC, shortening cycle time, increasing design traceability, or improving task completion performance. Once goals are identified, teams implement specific actions, such as enhancing code reviews, improving documentation standards, increasing test automation, or refining modeling practices. Metrics from earlier projects serve as the baseline against which new performance is measured. This continual comparison ensures that organizations do not simply repeat the same mistakes but evolve their practices, resulting in more predictable, higher-quality product development over time.

The Software Verification and Validation (V&V) Plan for the Encounter game project outlines both the internal and external processes that guide quality assurance throughout development. The plan begins with a clear statement of purpose, identifying the procedures that will validate requirements, verify design decisions, and confirm correct implementation. V&V activities are coordinated with each iteration of development, ensuring alignment with the project schedule described in the SPMP. The organization of the V&V effort assigns responsibilities to both the development team and a dedicated QA engineer. Development members perform verification tasks such as requirement inspections, design reviews, and unit testing using JUnit. External validation is handled partly through project engineers and partly through an independent QA resource. The plan also includes a Software Integrity Level Scheme, which categorizes the application’s required integrity level based on risk. Encounter is assessed as requiring moderate integrity, meaning validation conditions must be rigorous but manageable. Additionally, the V&V plan details acquisition verification, design verification, implementation checks, and system testing requirements. Structured questions guide each phase to ensure completeness, traceability, and compliance with standards. The combined processes ensure the final product meets both functional and quality expectations.

Nonfunctional requirements describe qualities or conditions the system must satisfy rather than specific behaviors. These include reliability, availability, performance, maintainability, portability, and security. For example, reliability requirements specify the ability of the software to behave consistently when subjected to the environment in which it is intended to operate. They limit the extent of imperfection by defining acceptable failure levels; for instance, an airport radar application may allow no more than two level-one faults per month. Availability requirements quantify the degree to which the system must be operational, sometimes expressed in “nines” such as 99.999% uptime. Performance requirements specify timing constraints, such as computation speed, throughput, or storage access. Security requirements describe protective measures like authentication, password rules, or behavior against malicious users. Maintainability refers to how easily the software can be modified or repaired. Portability describes the ability of the software to run on different platforms. Constraints further limit design freedom by defining required tools, platforms, accuracy expectations, or organizational standards. These limits are imposed by stakeholders or environments and must be respected throughout development.

A constraint on an application is a requirement that limits the available options for developing it. These nonfunctional requirements do not specify design details; instead, they define boundaries imposed by the customer, organizational rules, or external conditions. For example, design constraints may describe limits on how the system is to be implemented because stakeholders require them. Such constraints restrict the design freedom of developers. One example is that the Automobile Impact Facility software must utilize the Universal Crunch Form to display its results. Tool and language constraints are also common. These may reflect internal organizational practices, compatibility requirements, or programmer expertise. For instance, a project may specify that the software must be implemented in Java and developed on the Eclipse platform. Documentation constraints may be imposed by company or industry standards. In some cases, constraints arise from required hardware platforms or operating environments. These limitations must all be considered during project planning, as they affect the feasibility and complexity of the system. Projects are frequently constrained by available hardware resources, required memory, or storage space.

Projects are also frequently constrained by the hardware platforms they must use. For example, an application may be required to run on a specific workstation model with fixed memory or disk capacity. These hardware limits can significantly influence software design decisions, especially when dealing with performance-intensive or storage-heavy applications. It is essential for developers to understand these constraints early in the requirements phase to avoid costly redesigns later. Another important category of nonfunctional requirements concerns the external interfaces of the application. These include interfaces to other software, hardware, and communication systems. Requirements must specify how the application will interact with external components, including data formats, protocols, and timing constraints. User interface requirements often fall into this category as well. They define the look and feel of the system, the layout of screens, and the expected responsiveness to user actions. Finally, error-handling requirements describe how the system should respond to exceptional conditions, such as invalid input, hardware failure, or communication timeouts. These requirements ensure that the system behaves predictably and safely under unexpected circumstances.

The IEEE standard was developed and maintained by a committee of very experienced software engineers. It is very helpful, but it requires modification and tailoring to respond to changes in tools, languages, and practices. The first two sections of the standard, “Introduction” and “Overall description,” correspond to the high-level requirements and are covered in Chapter 11. Section 3 of the standard, the “Specific requirements,” corresponds to the detailed requirements. It is expanded and applied in Chapter 12.

Next we turn our attention to the essential links of the SRS to the rest of the project.

### 10.7 TRACEABILITY

Traceability is the ability to readily understand how the parts of separate project artifacts relate to each other. In particular, it links individual requirements with other project artifacts. (See, for example, [7].) A detailed requirement is traceable if there are clear links between it, the design element that accommodates it, the code that implements it, the inspection that verifies it, and the test that validates it. Figure 10.11 shows relationships between the artifacts, based on a single requirement.

Table 10.1 is an example of how a change in a requirement for DVDs in the video store application causes changes in the remaining artifacts.

Next we turn our attention to the essential links of the SRS to the rest of the project. Traceability is the ability to readily understand how the parts of separate project artifacts relate to each other. In particular, it links individual requirements with other project artifacts. A detailed requirement is traceable if there are clear links between it, the design element that accommodates it, the code that implements it, the inspection that verifies it, and the test that validates it. Figure 10.11 shows relationships between the artifacts, based on a single requirement. Table 10.1 is an example of how a change in a requirement for DVDs in the video store application causes changes in the remaining artifacts. Hyperlinking is a convenient way to transition easily between artifacts. In particular, the requirements document can be placed on the Internet or an intranet, and dependent artifacts can be connected via hyperlinks. Agile processes specify requirements analysis by first establishing a shared vision of the application. This is done via discussions among the team members and the customer. After that the requirements are gathered in relatively small stages. The vision stage is intended to form a description of the ultimate product that is simple and clear enough for all stakeholders to understand and relate to.

This vision is kept as concise as possible without compromising the shared vision itself. Examples are as follows: An application that allows video store personnel to manage DVD rentals. A Web-based calendar program for individuals and departments. A system that analyzes individuals’ susceptibility to disease based on their genetic information. The vision stage is followed by multiple iterations that are typically 2–4 weeks in duration. Such an iterative style has the advantage of keeping misunderstandings to a minimum and allowing all concerned to grapple with the requirements being considered. The requirements for each cycle are gathered by means of user stories—narratives, always told from the user’s perspective, of how the application is to be used. This process is summarized in Figure 10.12 and described in more detail in the sections that follow. The stories seek to identify the user, what the user wants the application to do, and the benefit of this action to the user. By keeping the stories short and in natural language, all project participants can understand them clearly. This improves communication, ensures correct understanding, and provides a foundation for planning design and implementation.

A typical user story might read as follows: “As a customer, I want to be able to check the availability of DVDs so that I can decide which one to rent.” Once these stories have been collected for a particular iteration, they are analyzed with respect to their complexity, implementation effort, and priority. The team assigns effort estimates using relative measures such as story points. These estimates help the project manager determine what can be reasonably implemented within the next iteration. After the stories are selected, the team elaborates them by identifying acceptance criteria—specific conditions that must be satisfied to consider the story successfully implemented. Acceptance criteria form the basis of test cases for verifying the functionality later in the cycle. During development, the team collaborates closely, frequently meeting to clarify ambiguities or refine details. Because each iteration is short, misunderstandings are caught early, and corrections can be made quickly. At the end of each iteration, the implemented stories are demonstrated to stakeholders. Feedback from this demonstration is incorporated into planning the following iteration, creating a continuous cycle of refinement and improvement throughout the project.

This iterative approach aligns well with the agile philosophy of incremental delivery and continuous improvement. It ensures that the most important requirements are tackled first and that the end product evolves in a way that aligns with user expectations. Moreover, because agile methods rely heavily on communication among team members, they reduce the overhead associated with detailed documentation. Instead of producing large specification documents up front, teams create lightweight artifacts that evolve alongside the software. This does not mean documentation is ignored; rather, it is created when needed and kept as concise as possible. Tools such as whiteboards, storyboards, and collaborative software help maintain shared understanding. Agile methods also emphasize the importance of testing throughout the lifecycle. Tests are written early, often before the code itself, in practices such as test-driven development. This ensures that development is guided by clear expectations and reduces the likelihood of defects. In addition, continuous integration systems automatically build and test the software whenever changes are made. By detecting problems early, these systems ensure that the software remains stable and that the team can move quickly without sacrificing quality.

Despite their advantages, agile methods are not without challenges. Because they depend heavily on effective communication, they can struggle in environments where team members are distributed geographically or do not share common working hours. Additionally, agile practices require a high degree of discipline; without it, teams may fail to maintain consistent quality or may lose track of priorities. Organizations with rigid structures or heavy reliance on documentation may find it difficult to transition to agile practices without cultural adjustments. Furthermore, stakeholders who are accustomed to detailed, up-front plans may feel uneasy with the flexible, iterative nature of agile development. To address these concerns, agile practitioners emphasize transparency: regular demonstrations, visible progress tracking, and open communication channels. Successful agile teams also invest time in retrospectives—meetings dedicated to identifying what went well, what could be improved, and what actions should be taken in the next cycle. These continuous process improvements help teams adapt quickly and maintain productivity. As projects grow in size and complexity, agile methods can be complemented with additional planning tools to ensure coordination across multiple teams.

At the early stages of requirements analysis, many issues remain unresolved because customers often do not clearly know what they want. They may express only general desires, leaving the details to be interpreted by the development team. For example, a customer may request a music application that allows novice users to write songs, but whether features such as auto-saving or tuning assistance are requirements depends on the agreement between customer and developer. Customers typically express what they want, not what they actually need, and it becomes the developer’s role to clarify those needs. This clarification often requires interviewing customers to understand their expectations. Different stakeholders may hold conflicting views about the final product, making it essential to identify whose needs have the highest priority. Understanding these perspectives early prevents costly redesigns later in the project. Overall, requirement clarification helps translate vague user wishes into concrete specifications, ensuring the system is both feasible and aligned with stakeholder goals.

Stakeholders are individuals or groups who have an interest in the outcome of a software product. In an e-commerce website, for instance, stakeholders include customers, marketing teams, developers, and even external organizations. Each stakeholder group may view the system differently depending on its goals. Customers may value ease of purchasing and reliability, while marketing teams may prioritize high-traffic features. Developers, on the other hand, might focus on maintainability and the ability to update the system efficiently. These differing perspectives can often lead to conflicting requirements. When conflicts arise, it becomes the responsibility of the requirements engineer to balance these interests and negotiate acceptable compromises. Additionally, shrink-wrapped software, such as mass-market office applications, introduces even more complexity because the “customer” is actually a composite of many end users with varying needs. In such scenarios, developers must create systems that satisfy broad expectations while staying within budget constraints. Understanding stakeholder vision early ensures smoother development and helps avoid later disputes about system functionality. Proper management of stakeholder expectations is therefore essential for successful software projects.

Interviewing customers is one of the most important techniques for gathering high-level requirements. Before the interview, the development team prepares by listing and prioritizing potential interviewees, typically focusing on those whose input will strongly influence the project’s success. Scheduling interviews with fixed start and end times helps maintain structure and efficiency. During the interview, the emphasis should be on active listening rather than leading the conversation. Developers are encouraged to probe gently, asking both open-ended and closed-ended questions. Open-ended questions, such as “What challenges do you face with the current system?” encourage detailed responses, while closed questions help confirm specific information. Taking thorough notes ensures that no detail is lost. After the interview, the team reviews notes, drafts high-level requirements, and prepares refined documentation for the customer to validate. Iterative interviews may follow to resolve ambiguities. A disciplined interview process builds a clearer understanding of what customers truly expect, reducing miscommunication and shaping more accurate requirement specifications.

Use cases are a widely used method for expressing functional requirements. They describe typical interactions between the user (called the actor) and the system, structured as a series of steps leading to a specific outcome. A basic use case includes a name, an actor, a main success scenario, and sometimes alternative or extension scenarios. For example, a word processor might include a “Retrieve a File” use case. The steps describe how the user clicks the file menu, selects an option, views directories, and opens a file. If an error occurs, such as selecting a file that cannot be opened, an extension scenario documents this exception. Use cases help clarify system behavior and highlight how different parts of the system interact with the user. They are especially useful because they are easy for both technical and non-technical stakeholders to understand. By capturing user intentions and system responses, use cases reduce ambiguity and ensure that essential user tasks are fully supported by the final application.

Use case diagrams provide a visual summary of interactions between actors and the system. Actors may include human users, external systems, or devices that communicate with the application. In the Encounter game example, actors include a player and a designer. The diagram shows the relationships between actors and different use cases such as “Initialize,” “Travel to Adjacent Area,” or “Set Rules.” Each connection line indicates which actor participates in which use case. While diagrams do not show detailed steps, they help stakeholders quickly understand the system’s scope. By examining these diagrams, developers can identify missing functionalities or overlapping responsibilities. They also help ensure that each actor’s needs are represented. Because they cover many interactions concisely, use case diagrams are ideal for early design discussions. They supplement written use case descriptions and serve as a bridge between requirements analysis and later system modeling.

The “Initialize” use case from the Encounter game illustrates how a system sets up the environment before interaction begins. It starts with the system displaying the player’s character in the dressing room. Then, the system provides a window for the user to assign qualities to the character, such as strength or intelligence. After setting these attributes, the player chooses an exit from the dressing room. The system then transitions the character into a new area on the opposite side of the exit. This simple use case outlines the sequence of actions required to start gameplay. Although the steps are straightforward, documenting them ensures that the development team understands the user’s expectations and the intended system behavior. The use case also supports testing, as each step can be converted into a test scenario. By clearly defining the initialization process, developers can maintain consistency across game sessions and provide a predictable, user-friendly experience.

The “Engage Foreign Character” use case describes how the player interacts with another character within the same game area. The process begins when the system displays the foreign character near the player’s character. Once both characters are visible, the system enables an exchange of quality values between them. These qualities may include attributes such as strength, intelligence, or other game-specific metrics. After this exchange, the system presents the results of the engagement, which determines how each character is affected by the interaction. Finally, the system relocates the player’s character to a different, randomly selected area. Documenting this use case clarifies the expected sequence of events during character encounters and ensures that developers implement consistent logic across all interactions. It also helps identify edge cases, such as what happens if a character lacks required attributes. By outlining these steps, the use case supports both development and testing teams in verifying that character interactions behave as intended within the game’s rules and mechanics.

Use cases can be categorized as sequential or orthogonal. Sequential use cases follow one another, with the output of one directly influencing the next. For example, in a financial application, a “reconcile account” use case may only occur after a user has selected “add checks.” This sequence ensures that interactions follow logical order. By contrast, orthogonal use cases represent entirely different views or operations. They do not depend on each other, and each addresses a distinct objective. For instance, a warehouse system may have orthogonal use cases for inventory analysis and for shipment scheduling. Both are crucial, yet they operate independently. In the Encounter game example, the “Set Rules” use case is orthogonal to “Engage Foreign Character,” because each concerns different aspects of gameplay. Understanding whether a use case is sequential or orthogonal helps prevent ambiguity in system design. It ensures that requirements are organized clearly and that developers correctly interpret how various system functions relate. This classification facilitates more effective requirement analysis and system modeling.

Agile projects handle high-level requirements differently from traditional methods. Instead of freezing requirements early, agile methodologies refine them continuously through customer collaboration. Requirements evolve in each iteration, allowing the team to adapt to new insights or changes in user needs. A key tool in agile development is the user story. Each user story represents a functional requirement written from the user’s perspective, describing what they need and why. User stories are typically small, discrete, and easy to estimate. They are also prioritized according to business value and customer importance. Unlike large, formal use case documents, user stories are simple and concise. They often include acceptance criteria that define when the story is complete. This clarity makes them effective for planning iterations and evaluating progress. Agile teams focus on delivering value incrementally, with customer feedback shaping the next steps. This dynamic approach reduces risk, minimizes misunderstanding, and ensures that the resulting system aligns more closely with what users truly want.

Security requirements are essential to ensure that a system protects data and maintains reliable operation. Common security requirements include confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorization, nonrepudiation, and availability. These requirements are often grouped under the CIA model—confidentiality, integrity, and availability. Confidentiality ensures that data is accessible only to authorized individuals, preventing exposure to unintended parties. Integrity guarantees that information remains unaltered unless changed by authorized actions. Authentication verifies the identity of a user or system, while authorization determines which actions or resources the user may access. Nonrepudiation ensures that a party cannot deny an action, often using mechanisms such as digital signatures. Availability ensures that the system remains accessible even during attacks, such as denial-of-service incidents. Together, these requirements form the foundation for designing secure systems. They help engineers anticipate potential vulnerabilities and plan appropriate countermeasures. Without proper attention to security requirements, systems may become susceptible to exploits that compromise sensitive information or disrupt critical operations.

The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) establishes strict requirements for protecting individuals’ medical information. It mandates that health data be created, stored, and transmitted securely by healthcare providers and organizations. HIPAA emphasizes confidentiality, integrity, and availability of health information, ensuring that it is used only for legitimate medical purposes. The law requires safeguards for physical storage, electronic transfer, maintenance, and authorized access. Any organization handling health information—such as hospitals, insurance companies, and clearinghouses—must comply with HIPAA’s standards. Noncompliance can lead to legal consequences, financial penalties, and loss of trust from patients. HIPAA highlights the importance of restricting access to sensitive data and ensuring that information remains accurate and protected from unauthorized modification. Its policies also address secure transmission across communication networks. By defining these high-level requirements, HIPAA has become a key framework guiding the design and operation of healthcare information systems, ensuring that patient data security is prioritized throughout medical processes.

Data flow diagrams (DFDs) are a useful technique for describing how information moves through a system. They represent processing elements, data stores, inputs, and outputs using standardized symbols such as circles and rectangles. Arrows indicate the flow of data between components. For example, in an ATM application, a DFD can show the steps involved in a deposit transaction—beginning when the user enters account information and ending when the system updates the account database. A DFD also reveals how data is displayed back to the user and which system components validate the information. By visualizing these interactions, DFDs help stakeholders understand the system’s structure without requiring detailed textual descriptions. They highlight where data originates, how it is processed, and where it ultimately resides. This clarity is especially valuable for identifying potential inefficiencies or missing data paths. Although DFDs do not show control flow or timing, they remain a powerful tool for high-level requirement analysis and communication among project teams.

State transition diagrams describe how an application changes from one state to another based on events. A state represents the condition or situation of a system at a particular moment. Transitions illustrate the events that trigger movement between states. For example, in a game application, the player may begin in a “Setting Up” state, where game elements are being initialized. When the player selects certain options, the system may move into a “Preparing” state, during which attributes are adjusted. A “Waiting” state can occur when no significant actions take place. Finally, the “Engaging” state represents active interaction, such as encountering another character. These diagrams allow developers and customers to share a clear understanding of how the system behaves dynamically. They are especially useful for event-driven applications such as games, real-time systems, or user interfaces. By mapping states and transitions, designers can identify missing events, handle exceptions more effectively, and ensure that the system responds predictably to user actions.

OpenOffice applications are designed to support a wide range of document-related tasks, including writing, creating spreadsheets, building presentations, and drawing diagrams. The requirements for OpenOffice emphasize ease of use, flexibility, and the ability to produce professional-quality output. For example, the Writer tool provides features such as spell checking, auto-completion, dictionary support, and formatting automation. These functions allow users to focus on content instead of layout. CALC supports data analysis through advanced formulas and charting tools, making it suitable for both simple and complex numerical tasks. IMPRESS enables users to create multimedia presentations with images, animation, and drawing tools. Additionally, the suite supports database connectivity, allowing users to handle structured information efficiently. High-level requirements for OpenOffice often highlight the need to generate complex documents quickly, integrate multiple tools seamlessly, and support a wide variety of file formats. By organizing these capabilities clearly, the requirements help ensure that the suite remains competitive and intuitive for everyday users.

Detailed requirements define the specific functionalities and constraints of a software system. Unlike high-level requirements, which describe broad goals, detailed requirements provide precise instructions for implementation. They include functional specifications, performance constraints, and user interface expectations. For example, a requirement may state that a system must calculate results in less than one second or that a character name cannot exceed fifteen letters. These details ensure consistency and guide developers during coding and testing. A well-organized collection of detailed requirements makes it easier to manage changes, locate specific information, and maintain documentation throughout a project’s lifecycle. Poorly structured requirement lists quickly become unmanageable, especially in large systems with hundreds of items. Detailed requirements also help prevent misunderstandings that could lead to costly mistakes. For instance, past engineering failures have occurred because teams used inconsistent units or made incorrect assumptions. By documenting every requirement precisely, teams reduce ambiguity and help ensure that the final system meets customer expectations accurately.

Organizing detailed requirements by feature is one of the oldest approaches in software engineering. This method involves grouping requirements according to the main functions an application provides. For example, in a video store management system, features might include checking in DVDs, stocking inventory, or registering customers. Each feature is defined by a stimulus and a corresponding system response. Although this method is simple and easy to understand, it has disadvantages. One drawback is that related requirements may become scattered across different sections of the specification. Changing one part of the application could require searching through multiple features to determine which other requirements are affected. Another issue is traceability—because requirements connected to the same object or data structure might be placed in unrelated sections. Nevertheless, feature-based organization remains useful for smaller systems or early stages of development. It provides a high-level view of the system’s capabilities and helps analysts identify gaps or missing functions. However, as the system grows, more structured organization styles may be necessary to maintain clarity.

Chapters 1 and 2 explain what operating systems are, what they do, and how they are designed and constructed. These chapters discuss what the common features of an operating system are and what an operating system does for the user. We include coverage of both traditional PC and server operating systems and operating systems for mobile devices. The presentation is motivational and explanatory in nature. We have avoided a discussion of how things are done internally in these chapters. Therefore, they are suitable for individual readers or for students in lower-level classes who want to learn what an operating system is without getting into the details of the internal algorithms. Process management. Chapters 3 through 5 describe the process concept and concurrency as the heart of modern operating systems. A process is the unit of work in a system. Such a system consists of a collection of concurrently executing processes, some executing operating-system code and others executing user code. These chapters cover methods for process scheduling and interprocess communication. Also included is a detailed discussion of threads, as well as an examination of issues related to multicore systems and parallel programming.

we have increased the coverage of process synchronization, we have divided the former Chapter 5 (Process Synchronization) into two separate chapters: Chapter 6, Synchronization Tools, and Chapter 7, Synchronization Examples. Memory management. Chapters 9 and 10 deal with the management of main memory during the execution of a process. To improve both the utilization of the CPU and the speed of its response to its users, the computer must keep several processes in memory. There are many different memory-management schemes, reflecting various approaches to memory management, and the effectiveness of a particular algorithm depends on the situation. Storage management.Chapters 11 and 12 describe how mass storage and I/O are handled in a modern computer system. The I/O devices that attach to a computer vary widely, and the operating system needs to provide a wide range of functionality to applications to allow them to control all aspects of these devices. We discuss system I/O in depth, including I/O system design, interfaces, and internal system structures and functions. In many ways, I/O devices are the slowest major components of the computer. Because they represent a performance bottleneck, we also examine performance issues associated with I/O devices.

Security and protection. Chapters 16 and 17 discuss the mechanisms necessary for the security and protection of computer systems. The processes in an operating system must be protected from one another's activities. To provide such protection, we must ensure that only processes that have gained proper authorization from the operating system can operate on the files, memory, CPU, and other resources of the system. Protection is a mechanism for controlling the access of programs, processes, or users to computer-system resources. This mechanism must provide a means of specifying the controls to be imposed, as well as a means of enforcement. Security protects the integrity of the information stored in the system (both data and code), as well as the physical resources of the system, from unauthorized access, malicious destruction or alteration, and accidental introduction of inconsistency. Advanced topics. Chapters 18 and 19 discuss virtual machines and networks/distributed systems. Chapter 18 provides an overview of virtual machines and their relationship to contemporary operating systems. Included is a general description of the hardware and software techniques that make virtualization possible. Chapter 19 provides an overview of computer networks and distributed systems, with a focus on the Internet and TCP/IP.

POSIX. POSIX (which stands for Portable Operating System Interface) represents a set of standards implemented primarily for UNIX-based operating systems. Although Windows systems can also run certain POSIX programs, our coverage of POSIX focuses on Linux and UNIX systems. POSIX-compliant systems must implement the POSIX core standard (POSIX.1); Linux and macOS are examples of POSIX-compliant systems. POSIX also defines several extensions to the standards, including real-time extensions (POSIX.1b) and an extension for a threads library (POSIX.1c, better known as Pthreads). We provide several programming examples written in C illustrating the POSIX base API, as well as Pthreads and the extensions for real-time programming. These example programs were tested on Linux 4.4 and macOS 10.11 systems using the gcc compiler. Java. Java is a widely used programming language with a rich API and built-in language support for concurrent and parallel programming. Java programs run on any operating system supporting a Java virtual machine (or JVM). We illustrate various operating-system and networking concepts with Java programs tested using Version 1.8 of the Java Development Kit (JDK).

Windows systems. The primary programming environment for Windows systems is the Windows API, which provides a comprehensive set of functions for managing processes, threads, memory, and peripheral devices. We supply a modest number of C programs illustrating the use of this API. Programs were tested on a system running Windows 10. We have chosen these three programming environments because we believe that they best represent the two most popular operating-system models—Linux/UNIX and Windows—along with the widely used Java environment. Most programming examples are written in C, and we expect readers to be comfortable with this language. Readers familiar with both the C and Java languages should easily understand most programs provided in this text. In some instances—such as thread creation—we illustrate a specific concept using all three programming environments, allowing the reader to contrast the three different libraries as they address the same task. In other situations, we may use just one of the APIs to demonstrate a concept. For example, we illustrate shared memory using just the POSIX API; socket programming in TCP/IP is highlighted using the Java API.

As we wrote this Tenth Edition of Operating System Concepts, we were guided by the sustained growth in four fundamental areas that affect operating systems: 1. Mobile operating systems 2. Multicore systems 3. Virtualization 4. Nonvolatile memory secondary storage. To emphasize these topics, we have integrated relevant coverage throughout this new edition. For example, we have greatly increased our coverage of the Android and iOS mobile operating systems, as well as our coverage of the ARMv8 architecture that dominates mobile devices. We have also increased our coverage of multicore systems, including increased coverage of APIs that provide support for concurrency and parallelism. Nonvolatile memory devices like SSDs are now treated as the equals of hard-disk drives in the chapters that discuss I/O, mass storage, and file systems. Several of our readers have expressed support for an increase in Java coverage, and we have provided additional Java examples throughout this edition. Additionally, we have rewritten material in almost every chapter by bringing older material up to date and removing material that is no longer interesting or relevant.

Major Changes. The Tenth Edition update encompasses much more material than previous updates, in terms of both content and new supporting material. Next, we provide a brief outline of the major content changes in each chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction includes updated coverage of multicore systems, as well as new coverage of NUMA systems and Hadoop clusters. Old material has been updated, and new motivation has been added for the study of operating systems. Chapter 2: Operating-System Structures provides a significantly revised discussion of the design and implementation of operating systems. We have updated our treatment of Android and iOS and have revised our coverage of the system boot process with a focus on GRUB for Linux systems. New coverage of the Windows subsystem for Linux is included as well. We have added new sections on linkers and loaders, and we now discuss why applications are often operating-system specific. Finally, we have added a discussion of the BCC debugging toolset. Chapter 3: Processes simplifies the discussion of scheduling so that it now includes only CPU scheduling issues. New coverage describes the memory layout of a C program, the Android process hierarchy, Mach message passing, and Android RPCs. We have also replaced coverage of the traditional UNIX/Linux init process with coverage of systemd.

Chapter 4: Threads and Concurrency (previously Threads) increases the coverage of support for concurrent and parallel programming at the API and library level. We have revised the section on Java threads so that it now includes futures and have updated the coverage of Apple’s Grand Central Dispatch so that it now includes Swift. New sections discuss fork-join parallelism using the fork-join framework in Java, as well as Intel thread building blocks. Chapter 5: CPU Scheduling (previously Chapter 6) revises the coverage of multilevel queue and multicore processing scheduling. We have integrated coverage of NUMA-aware scheduling issues throughout, including how this scheduling affects load balancing. We also discuss related modifications to the Linux CFS scheduler. New coverage combines discussions of round-robin and priority scheduling, heterogeneous multiprocessing, and Windows 10 scheduling.

Chapter 6: Synchronization Tools (previously part of Chapter 5, Process Synchronization) focuses on various tools for synchronizing processes. Significant new coverage discusses architectural issues such as instruction reordering and delayed writes to buffers. The chapter also introduces lock-free algorithms using compare-and-swap (CAS) instructions. No specific APIs are presented; rather, the chapter provides an introduction to race conditions and general tools that can be used to prevent data races. Details include new coverage of memory models, memory barriers, and liveness issues. Chapter 7: Synchronization Examples (previously part of Chapter 5, Process Synchronization) introduces classical synchronization problems and discusses specific API support for designing solutions that solve these problems. The chapter includes new coverage of POSIX named and unnamed semaphores, as well as condition variables. A new section on Java synchronization is included as well.

Chapter 9: Memory-Management Strategies (previously Main Memory) and Chapter 10: Virtual-Memory Management (previously Virtual Memory) have been retitled and reorganized. The two chapters now contain numerous updates related to modern architectures, including ARMv8 and x86-64. The coverage of paging has been significantly revised, including new coverage on hierarchical paging, hashed page tables, and inverted page tables. New sections have been added describing virtual memory in Linux and Windows 10. Our discussion of segmentation has been updated to focus on Intel’s x86-64 architecture. New coverage of demand paging includes an examination of instruction reordering and copy-on-write. Chapter 10 includes new coverage of the prepaging technique. Additionally, coverage of thrashing has been enhanced, and a discussion of kernel memory allocation and slab allocators is now included. Finally, we discuss how Linux, Windows 10, and macOS implement virtual memory.

Chapter 11: Mass-Storage Structure (previously Mass-Storage Structure) includes significant updates to disk-scheduling algorithms, including LOOK and CLOOK. The chapter also includes new coverage of NVM-aware RAID systems and memory-mapped I/O. Our discussion of solid-state drives (SSDs) has been greatly expanded and updated. Chapter 12: I/O Systems (previously I/O Systems) provides a revised general overview of the I/O system, including a discussion of polling and DMA. The chapter includes new coverage of I/O performance issues, including application I/O, kernel I/O, and caching. New sections on I/O schedulers in Linux and Windows 10, as well as kernel I/O subsystem and network block devices, are included. Finally, the chapter includes a discussion of streams. Throughout both chapters, we have updated and enhanced our coverage of I/O-related issues, particularly as they pertain to nonvolatile memory and mobile systems.

Chapter 8: Deadlocks (previously Chapter 7) provides minor updates, including a new section on livelock and a discussion of deadlock as an example of a liveness hazard. The chapter includes new coverage of the Linux lockdep and the BCC deadlock detector tools, as well as coverage of Java deadlock detection using thread dumps. Chapter 9: Main Memory (previously Chapter 8) includes several revisions that bring the chapter up to date with respect to memory management on modern computer systems. We have added new coverage of the ARMv8 64-bit architecture, updated the coverage of dynamic link libraries, and changed swapping coverage so that it now focuses on swapping pages rather than processes. We have also eliminated coverage of segmentation. Chapter 10: Virtual Memory (previously Chapter 9) contains several revisions, including updated coverage of memory allocation on NUMA systems and global allocation using a free-frame list. New coverage includes compressed memory, major/minor page faults, and memory management in Linux and Windows 10.

Chapter 13: File-System Interface (previously Chapter 11) has been updated with information about current technologies. In particular, the coverage of directory structures has been improved, and the coverage of protection has been updated. The memory-mapped files section has been expanded, and a Windows API example has been added to the discussion of shared memory. The ordering of topics is refactored in Chapter 13 and 14. Chapter 14: File-System Implementation (previously Chapter 12) has been updated with coverage of current technologies. The chapter now includes discussions of TRIM and the Apple File System. In addition, the discussion of performance has been updated, and the coverage of journaling has been expanded. Chapter 15: File System Internals is new and contains updated information from previous Chapters 11 and 12. Chapter 16: Security (previously Chapter 15) now precedes the protection chapter.

Chapter 16: Security (previously Chapter 15) now precedes the protection chapter. It includes revised and updated terms for current security threats and solutions, including ransomware and remote access tools. The principle of least privilege is emphasized. Coverage of code-injection vulnerabilities and attacks has been revised and now includes code samples. Discussion of encryption technologies has been updated to focus on the technologies currently used. Coverage of authentication (by passwords and other methods) has been updated and expanded with helpful hints. Additions include a discussion of address-space layout randomization and a new summary of security defenses. The Windows 7 example has been updated to Windows 10. Chapter 17: Protection (previously Chapter 14) contains major changes. The discussion of protection rings and layers has been updated and now refers to the Bell–LaPadula model and explores the ARM model of TrustZones and Secure Monitor Calls.

The discussion of protection rings and layers has been updated and now refers to the Bell–LaPadula model and explores the ARM model of TrustZones and Secure Monitor Calls. Coverage of the need-to-know principle has been expanded, as has coverage of mandatory access control. Subsections on Linux capabilities, Darwin entitlements, security integrity protection, system-call filtering, sandboxing, and code signing have been added. Coverage of run-time-based enforcement in Java has also been added, including the stack inspection technique. Chapter 18: Virtual Machines (previously Chapter 16) includes added details about hardware assistance technologies. Also expanded is the topic of application containment, now including containers, zones, docker, and Kubernetes. A new section discusses ongoing virtualization research, including unikernels, library operating systems, partitioning hypervisors, and separation hypervisors.

Chapter 19, Networks and Distributed Systems (previously Chapter 17) has been substantially updated and now combines coverage of computer networks and distributed systems. The material has been revised to bring it up to date with respect to contemporary computer networks and distributed systems. The TCP/IP model receives added emphasis, and a discussion of cloud storage has been added. The section on network topologies has been removed. Coverage of name resolution has been expanded and a Java example added. The chapter also includes new coverage of distributed file systems, including MapReduce on top of Google file system, Hadoop, GPFS, and Lustre. Chapter 20: The Linux System (previously Chapter 18) has been updated to cover the Linux 4.i kernel. Chapter 21: The Windows 10 System is a new chapter that covers the internals of Windows 10. Appendix A: Influential Operating Systems has been updated to include material from chapters that are no longer covered in the text.

Operating systems are an essential part of any computer system. Similarly, a course on operating systems is an essential part of any computer science education. This field is undergoing rapid change, as computers are now prevalent in virtually every arena of day-to-day life-from embedded devices in auto- mobiles through the most sophisticated planning tools for governments and multinational firms. Yet the fundamental concepts remain fairly clear, and it is on these that we base this book. We wrote this book as a text for an introductory course in operating sys- tems at the junior or senior undergraduate level or at the first-year graduate level. We hope that practitioners will also find it useful. It provides a clear description of the concepts that underlie operating systems. As prerequisites, we assume that the reader is familiar with basic data structures, computer organization, and a high-level language, such as C or Java. The hardware topics required for an understanding of operating systems are covered in Chapter 1. In that chapter, we also include an overview of the fundamental data structures that are prevalent in most operating systems. For code examples, we use pre- dominantly C, as well as a significant amount of Java, but the reader can still understand the algorithms without a thorough knowledge of these languages.

Concepts are presented using intuitive descriptions. Important theoretical results are covered, but formal proofs are largely omitted. The bibliographical notes at the end of each chapter contain pointers to research papers in which results were first presented and proved, as well as references to recent material for further reading. In place of proofs, figures and examples are used to suggest why we should expect the result in question to be true. The fundamental concepts and algorithms covered in the book are often based on those used in both open-source and commercial operating systems. Our aim is to present these concepts and algorithms in a general setting that is not tied to one particular operating system. However, we present a large number of examples that pertain to the most popular and the most innovative operating systems, including Linux, Microsoft Windows, Apple macOS (the original name, OS X, was changed in 2016 to match the naming scheme of other Apple products), and Solaris. We also include examples of both Android and iOS, currently the two dominant mobile operating systems.

The organization of the text reflects our many years of teaching courses on operating systems. Consideration was also given to the feedback provided by the reviewers of the text, along with the many comments and suggestions we received from readers of our previous editions and from our current and former students. This Tenth Edition also reflects most of the curriculum guide- lines in the operating-systems area in Computer Science Curricula 2013, the most recent curriculum guidelines for undergraduate degree programs in computer science published by the IEEE Computing Society and the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM).
